note:
巴黎已经选择了电动滑板车运营商。多亏了市长安妮·伊达尔戈(Anne Hidalgo)富有远见的领导，这座城市迅速成为骑自行车、步行和滑板车的避风港，并选择Lime、Tier和Dott作为其滑板车公司。每家公司都将获得一份为期两年的合同，允许其在全市部署5000辆电动滑板车，总计1.5万辆滑板车。
对于总部位于旧金山的Lime公司来说，这一结果代表着一次巨大的胜利。该公司的主要投资者包括优步(Uber)和谷歌(Google)。对于Lime的主要竞争对手Bird来说，这也是一个惨痛的损失。Bird是一家共享滑板车的先驱，该公司曾计划通过雇佣数千人在位于巴黎的新欧洲中心工作，将巴黎作为其“第二个家”。伯德的发言人没有立即回复记者的置评请求。
这一结果对莱姆来说是一个巨大的胜利，但对伯德来说也是一个惨痛的损失。
电动滑板车可以通过智能手机应用程序租赁，2018年首次出现在巴黎街头。但摩托车的涓涓细流很快变成了洪水，导致大约2万辆摩托车淹没了街道。伊达尔戈称这些滑板车是“无政府状态”，而交通部长伊丽莎白·伯恩告诉“巴黎人报”，这座城市正在经历“弱肉强食”。
去年，该市宣布了一项计划，通过选择三家官方运营商来控制混乱。伊达尔戈在2019年表示：“我们需要秩序和规则来确保道路安全，让我们城市的街道、人行道和社区平静下来。”

-安妮·伊达尔戈(@Anne_Hidalgo)2020年7月23日。
总共有16家滑板车共享公司回应了该市的提案请求。莱姆是唯一一家赢得合同的美国公司，该公司表示“很高兴能成为获胜者之一”。总部设在柏林的Tier赢得了“世界上最大的电动滑板车招标”。总部位于阿姆斯特丹的多特在推特上发布了一张其团队庆祝的照片。
伊达尔戈最近几乎完全凭借一个将汽车从城市中移除以促进骑自行车和步行的平台赢得连任，他说，选择滑板车运营商是基于三个标准：环境责任，对用户的安全，以及管理滑板车的维护和充电。
她还呼吁骑摩托车的人尊重道路规则，同时指出，巴黎各地正在安装2500个专门的摩托车停车位。
选择巴黎的滑板车运营商可能会对欧洲和美国的微型移动行业产生深远影响，特别是在冠状病毒大流行正在推动更多合并和整合的情况下。NGP Capital的管理合伙人、Lime的投资者保罗·阿塞尔(Paul Asel)在6月份告诉“商业内幕”(Business Insider)，巴黎的决定可能只会为未来只有两三家在欧洲运营的公司铺平道路。
最初，新冠肺炎让共享滑板车行业陷入停顿。随着滑板车公司将他们的车辆从城市街道上拖走，乘客量直线下降。按机队规模和估值计算，鸟牌和Lime是最大的两家公司，近几个月来经历了大规模裁员，取消了约580个全职职位。优步出售了其共享自行车和滑板车业务Jump on Lime，这是一项投资的一部分，这项投资将使Lime的估值下降近80%。
但该行业正在缓慢复苏，这要归功于人们对骑自行车和其他替代交通方式的兴趣的复苏。随着城市居民寻找安全的出行方式，他们也在长途旅行中使用滑板车。上周，Lime报告称，自大流行开始以来，滑板车旅行的持续时间平均延长了34%，距离延长了18%。
note_en:
Paris has chosen its electric scooter operators. The city, which has quickly become  a haven for biking, walking, and scootering thanks to the forward-thinking leadership of Mayor Anne Hidalgo, has selected Lime, Tier, and Dott as its scooter companies. Each company will be awarded a two-year contract which allows it to deploy 5,000 electric scooters throughout the city, for a total of 15,000 scooters.
 The results represent a huge win for Lime, the San Francisco-based company that counts Uber and Google among its major investors. It’s also a stinging loss for Lime’s main rival, Bird, the scooter sharing pioneer that had plans to  make Paris its “second home” by hiring thousands of people to work at its new European hub to be located in the city. A spokesperson for Bird didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment.
 The results represent a huge win for Lime, but also a stinging loss for Bird
 Electric scooters, which are available for rent through a smartphone app, first appeared on the streets of Paris in 2018. But the trickle of scooters soon turned into a deluge, resulting in approximately 20,000 scooters inundating the streets. Hidalgo described the scooters as “anarchic,” while transport minister Élisabeth Borne told  Le Parisien the city was experiencing “the law of the jungle.”
 Last year, the city announced a plan to bring the chaos under control by selecting three official operators. “We need order and rules to assure road safety and to calm the streets, sidewalks and neighborhoods of our city,” Hidalgo said in 2019.
 Toutes mes félicitations à  @limebike,  @dott_fr et  @tierscooters, lauréats de l’appel d’offre trottinette lancé par  @Paris. Ces 3 opérateurs seront les seuls à pouvoir déployer chacun 5.000 trottinettes maximum dans Paris.  cc  @David_Belliard  1/3  https://t.co/NewayZ8Usi
— Anne Hidalgo (@Anne_Hidalgo)  July 23, 2020
 In total, 16 scooter sharing companies responded to the city’s request for proposals. Lime, the only US-based company to win a contract,  said it was “thrilled to be among the winners.” Tier, which is headquartered in Berlin,  crowed by winning “the world’s biggest e-scooter tender.” And Dott, based in Amsterdam,  posted a photo of its team celebrating on Twitter.
 Hidalgo, who recently won reelection almost exclusively on a platform of removing cars from cities to promote biking and walking, said that scooter operators were selected based on three criteria: environmental responsibility, safety for users, and managing the maintenance and recharging of scooters.
 She also called on scooter riders to respect the rules of the road, while noting that 2,500 dedicated parking spaces for scooters are being installed throughout Paris.
  The selection of Paris’ scooter operators could have a profound effect on the micromobility industry, both in Europe and the US, especially as the coronavirus pandemic is driving more mergers and consolidation. Paul Asel, managing partner at NGP Capital and an investor in Lime,  told  Business Insider in June that Paris’ decision would likely set the stage for only two or three companies operating in Europe going forward.
 Initially,  COVID-19 brought the scooter sharing industry to a halt. Ridership plummeted as scooter companies yanked their vehicles from city streets.  Bird and  Lime, the two biggest companies in terms of fleet size and valuation, went through mass layoffs in recent months, eliminating around 580 full-time positions.  Uber offloaded its shared bike and scooter business Jump on Lime as part of an investment that would see Lime’s valuation drop by nearly 80 percent.
 But the industry is slowly recovering, thanks to a resurgence in interest in biking and other alternate forms of transportation. As they look for safe ways to get around, city dwellers are using scooters for longer trips too. Last week, Lime reported that on average  scooter trips have been 34 percent longer in duration and 18 percent farther in distance since the pandemic started.
title: 巴黎选择Lime、Tier和Dott作为其电动滑板车运营商，冷落了Bird，后者曾计划在两年内建立一个巴黎枢纽并雇佣1000名员工
title_en: Paris selects Lime, Tier, and Dott as its electric scooter operators, snubbing Bird, which had planned to build a Paris hub and hire 1,000 people over two years

note:
Butlr是麻省理工学院媒体实验室的一个分支，它使用无线、电池供电的硬件和人工智能相结合的方式来跟踪人们在室内的活动，而不会侵犯他们的隐私。这家创业公司使用安装在天花板上的传感器来检测个人的体温，以跟踪一个人走到哪里，以及他们下一步可能去哪里。用例几乎是无穷无尽的。传感器可以在检测到运动时打开情绪照明或空调，帮助企业了解购物者如何导航商店，确定结账处排队的时间，甚至在下班后检测到有人时发出警报。
通过使用被动红外传感器只探测体温，传感器不知道你是谁-只知道你在哪里，你要去哪里。一旦你离开传感器的范围，跟踪就会停止，就像你离开商店一样。
这项技术需求量很大。巴特勒说，大约有20万家零售店使用它的技术，特别是因为它比监控摄像头和面部识别等更侵犯隐私的-也更昂贵的-替代品便宜得多。
但当大流行来袭时，这些商店中的大多数都关闭了-整个城市和国家实际上也是如此-以应对来自新冠肺炎的持续威胁。但这些商店将不得不重新开张，因此巴特勒重新开始工作。
Butlr的联合创始人邓洪浩告诉TechCrunch，该公司开始重组其技术，以帮助支持门店重新开张。
该公司迅速推出了新的软件功能-如最大入住率和队列管理-以帮助已经安装了传感器的商店应对新的但不断变化的法律和指导方针，这些法律和指导方针是企业必须遵守的。
邓说，传感器可以确保一次在商店里的人数不超过允许的人数，并通过帮助执行社会距离规则来确保员工免受顾客的伤害。邓说，顾客还可以看到实时排队数据，帮助他们挑选一个不那么拥挤的时间购物。
坦率地说，在大流行之前，所有这些事情听起来可能有点乏味。快进到大流行的中期，你可能会对你能得到的所有帮助和技术表示感谢。
Butlr在2月份疫情上升的高峰期在中国测试了它的新功能，后来向包括美国在内的全球客户推出。邓说，Butlr的技术已经在帮助家具店Steelcase、连锁超市99 Ranch Market和阿布扎比卢浮宫博物馆的顾客帮助他们重新开业，同时将对其他人的风险降至最低。
这是一个得到回报的轴心。上个月，巴特勒筹集了120万美元的种子资金，当时美国的大流行正达到顶峰。
没有人知道一场大流行即将到来，尤其是邓。随着大流行的蔓延，企业受到了影响。如果不是因为思维敏捷，Butlr可能是另一家在大流行中屈服的初创公司。
相反，这家初创公司可能会帮助拯救生命-而且不会损害任何人的隐私。
note_en:
Butlr, a spin-out of the MIT Media Lab, uses a  mix of wireless, battery-powered hardware and artificial intelligence to track people’s movements indoors without violating their privacy. The startup uses ceiling-mounted sensors to detect individuals’ body heat to track where a person walks and where they might go next. The use-cases are near-endless. The sensors can turn on mood-lighting or air conditioning when it detects movement, help businesses understand how shoppers navigate their stores, determine the wait-time in the queues at the checkout, and even sound the alarm if it detects a person after-hours.
 By using passive infrared sensors to detect only body heat, the sensors don’t know who you are — only where you are and where you’re heading. The tracking stops as soon as you leave the sensor’s range, like when you leave a store.
 The technology is in high demand. Butlr says some 200,000 retail stores use its technology, not least because it’s far cheaper than the more privacy-invading — and expensive — alternatives, like surveillance cameras and facial recognition.
 But when the pandemic hit, most of those stores closed — as effectively did entire cities and nations — to counter the ongoing threat from of COVID-19. But those stores would have to open again, and so Butlr got back to work.
  Butlr’s co-founder Honghao Deng told TechCrunch that it began retooling its technology to help support stores opening again.
 The company quickly rolled out new software features — like maximum occupancy and queue management — to help stores with sensors already installed  cope with the new but ever-changing laws and guidance that businesses had to comply with.
 Deng said that the sensors can make sure no more than the allowed number of people can be in a store at once, and make sure that staff are protected from customers by helping to enforce social distancing rules. Customers can also see live queue data to help them pick a less-crowded time to shop, said Deng.
 All these things before a pandemic might have sounded, frankly, a little dull. Fast forward to the middle of a pandemic and you’re probably thankful for all the help — and the technology — you can get.
 Butlr tested its new features in China at the height of the pandemic’s rise in February, and later rolled out to its global customers, including in the United States. Deng said Butlr’s technology is already helping customers at furniture store Steelcase, supermarket chain 99 Ranch Market, and the Louvre Museum in Abu Dhabi to help them reopen while minimizing the risk to others.
 It’s a pivot that’s paid off. Last month Butlr raised $1.2 million in seed funding, just as the pandemic was reaching its peak in the United States.
 Nobody knew a pandemic was coming, not least Deng. And as the pandemic spread, businesses have suffered. If it wasn’t for quick thinking, Butlr might’ve been another startup that succumbed to the pandemic.
 Instead, the startup is probably going to help save lives — and without compromising anyone’s privacy.
title: 这家初创公司对其隐私友好型传感器进行了改造，以帮助对抗新冠肺炎
title_en: This startup reworked its privacy-friendly sensors to help battle COVID-19

总部位于伊利诺伊州芝加哥的亚洲外卖平台Chowbus在首轮融资中筹集了3300万美元。
这轮融资由Altos Ventures牵头，Left Lane Capital有海德公园天使公司(Hyde Park Angels)、菲卡风险投资公司(Fika Ventures)、FJ Labs和硅谷银行(Silicon Valley Bank)参与。
该公司将利用新的资金继续增长其现有业务，在新城市推出其平台，并扩大其产品线，同时继续支持该平台上的餐厅和客户。
Chowbus由张素玉和温林欣于2016年创立，是一个外卖平台，将食客与北美20多个城市的夫妻店和商店的正宗亚洲食物联系起来。双语平台提供精选的餐厅列表，配以图片，并提供“捆绑”功能，允许用户在一次送货中从多家餐厅订购最好的菜肴。
这款应用程序在纽约、波士顿、费城、芝加哥、亚特兰大、洛杉矶、旧金山湾区、西雅图和北美其他许多城市都有售。
该公司还增加了跳跃自行车的前首席运营官(COO)和优步首批50名员工之一蔡崇信(Kenny Tsai)担任首席运营官，前Groupon产品负责人郑杰英(音译)担任产品主管。
note_en:
Chowbus, a Chicago, IL-based Asian food delivery platform, raised $33m in Series A funding round.
  The round was led by Altos Ventures and Left Lane Capital with participation from Hyde Park Angels, Fika Ventures, FJ Labs and Silicon Valley Bank.
 The company will use the new funding to continue to grow its existing business, launch its platform in new cities, and expand its product line, while continuing to support the restaurants and customers on the platform.
 Founded in 2016 by Suyu Zhang and Linxin Wen, Chowbus is a food-delivery platform that connects diners with authentic Asian food from “mom and pop” restaurants and stores in over 20 cities in North America. The bilingual platform provides a curated list of restaurants paired with images and a “bundling” feature that allows users to order the best dishes from multiple restaurants in a single delivery.
 The app is available in New York, Boston, Philadelphia, Chicago, Atlanta, Los Angeles, the Bay Area, Seattle, and many other cities across North America.
 The company has also added former Chief Operating Officer (COO) of JUMP Bikes and one of Uber’s first 50 employees, Kenny Tsai, as COO, and former Groupon product leader Jieying Zheng as Head of Product.
title: 总部位于芝加哥的外卖服务公司Chowbus将食客与北美20多个城市的亚洲餐厅联系起来，筹集了3300万美元的首轮融资
title_en: Chicago-based food delivery service Chowbus, which connects diners with Asian restaurants in over 20 cities in North America, raises $33M Series A

note:
畅销书提供了一扇窗口，让人们了解消费它们的文化的思想和习惯，当它们仍然很受欢迎时，它们就像是现在和过去之间的桥梁，创造了一个想象中的读者社区，他们一直在进行同样的阅读旅程，拥有一套共同的兴趣或想法。
但是，许多曾经接触到广大读者的书今天已经不再被阅读了。这些被遗忘的畅销书可以向我们展示世界发生了怎样的变化，让我们得以一窥我们与过去的读者的不同之处-我们的世界与他们的世界有何不同，品味和习惯发生了怎样的变化，出版方式发生了怎样的变化。这些书经常绝版，主要在古腾堡计划(Project Gutenberg)等网站和二手书店可以买到，它们提供了一个独特的优势，可以用来观察特定时刻的阅读习惯。
哈丽特·比彻·斯托(Harriet Beecher Stowe)1852年的小说“汤姆叔叔的小屋”是19世纪最畅销的小说。它催生了翻译、续集、舞台版本、恶搞和致敬，并继续被阅读、记忆和审问。在斯托出版她的书之前，美国最畅销的小说是苏珊娜·罗森(Susana Rowson)的“夏洛特·坦普尔”(Charlotte Temple，1794年)，这本书没有那么令人难忘，尽管它出现了200多个版本，并且在100多年里一直很受欢迎。几十年来，它激发了人们前往纽约三一教堂后面的墓地朝圣的热情，在那里可以找到虚构主人公的墓碑。(今天的朝圣者更有可能是在寻找埋在教堂墓地另一部分的亚历山大·汉密尔顿(Alexander Hamilton)。)。曾经流行但现在被遗忘的书籍最终被排除在“古典文学”的典范之外。这些书中有许多是由妇女和有色人种写的。第一次世界大战后，夏洛特·坦普尔(Charlotte Temple)失宠，因为人们的品味发生了变化，文学学者从通俗文学转向了最初出版时往往绝对不受欢迎的书籍，比如《白鲸》(Moby Dick)。
为了深入研究我们可以从被遗忘的畅销书中学到什么，我们选择了一本今天几乎从未读过或讨论过的畅销小说：玛丽·奥古斯塔·沃德(Mary Augusta Ward)的罗斯夫人的女儿。我们正在出版这本小说的新电子版，这是1903年在美国最畅销的书，并附有注释、当代评论和对文本的其他回应。
玛丽·沃德是一位英国作家，她以结婚后的名字汉弗莱·沃德夫人出版，是20世纪初最受欢迎的英语小说家之一。1881年至1920年间，她出版了26部小说，其中许多是畅销书。但到了20世纪20年代，她的许多小说，甚至是那些非常受欢迎的小说，都已经绝版。今天，她很少在学术界之外被阅读。“罗斯夫人的女儿”是一部关于私生子、背叛和女性在社会中的角色的社会浪漫故事，在第一次出版时就吸引了许多读者的想象力。通过今天的阅读，我们既可以欣赏一个好故事本身，也可以试图理解是什么迫使这么多人为了有机会在沃德创造的虚构世界中度过一段时间而支付来之不易的1.5美元。
我们邀请读者在社交媒体上使用#LadyRoseLQ标签讨论罗斯夫人的女儿，并加入我们在线出版平台Manifold上的阅读小组，在那里你可以使用注释和评论功能来讨论这部小说。要加入我们，您必须首先通过选择“需要注册？”在此创建帐户。创建帐户后，您可以通过此链接连接到讨论。您也可以为您的朋友、读书俱乐部或班级创建您自己的阅读小组。(有关如何做到这一点的详细信息，请单击此处。)。
在接下来的两周里，我们将发表一系列文章，通过探索过去畅销书排行榜上的各种作品，挑战我们对通俗文学的常见概念-我们甚至会深入研究这些榜单最初是如何编制的。这些散文涉及的主题从俄罗斯第一本现代畅销书到伊迪丝·沃顿(Edith Wharton)对畅销书的鄙视，尽管她自己也是一名畅销小说家。我们还将发表一篇关于言情小说史前史的文章，研究反奴隶制的大众媒体，以及探索更广泛的印刷和图书出版历史的信息图。我们的目标是为创作和阅读流行作品的文化提供新的见解。为什么有些书被证明是永恒的，而另一些书似乎只在当时的读者中引起了共鸣，尽管是强烈的共鸣？

散文·讨厌畅销书的畅销书希拉·利明·嫁给了艾米丽·哈内特的莫姆主义
note_en:
Best sellers offer a window into the thoughts and habits of the cultures that consumed them, and when they remain popular they act as a bridge between the present and the past, creating an imagined community of readers who have been on the same reading journey and who share a set of interests or ideas.
 But many books that once reached a wide audience are no longer read today. These forgotten best sellers can show us the ways in which the world has changed, offering a glimpse into what makes us different from the readers of the past—how our world is distinct from theirs, how tastes and habits have altered, how publishing practices have shifted. These books, often out-of-print and available mainly on sites like Project Gutenberg and in used bookstores, present a unique vantage from which to observe the reading habits of a particular moment.
 Harriet Beecher Stowe’s 1852 novel  Uncle Tom’s Cabin was the best-selling novel of the nineteenth century. It spawned translations, sequels, stage versions, parodies, and homages, and it continues to be read, remembered, and interrogated. Before Stowe published her book, the best-selling novel in the United States was Susana Rowson’s  Charlotte Temple (1794), a book nowhere near as well remembered despite the fact that it appeared in over two hundred editions and remained popular for more than a hundred years. For decades, it inspired pilgrimages to the graveyard behind Trinity Church in New York, where a gravestone for the fictional protagonist can be found. (Today’s pilgrims are much more likely to be looking for  Alexander Hamilton, buried in another part of the churchyard.) Books that were once popular but now forgotten wind up excluded from the canon of “classic” literature. Many of these books were written by women and people of color.  Charlotte Temple fell out of favor after World War I, as tastes changed and literary scholars turned away from popular literature to books that were often decidedly unpopular when first published, like  Moby Dick.
 To make an intimate study of what we can learn from forgotten bestsellers, we have selected a best-selling novel that is today almost never read or discussed: Mary Augusta Ward’s  Lady Rose’s Daughter. We are publishing  a new digital edition of the novel, the best-selling book of 1903 in the United States, accompanied by annotations, contemporary reviews, and other responses to the text.
  Mary Ward, a British writer who published under her married name, Mrs. Humphry Ward, was one of the most popular English-language novelists of the turn of the twentieth century. Between 1881 and 1920 she published twenty-six novels, many of them best sellers. But by the 1920s many of her novels, even those that had been remarkably popular, had fallen out of print. Today she is rarely read outside academic circles.  Lady Rose’s Daughter, a society romance about illegitimacy, betrayal, and the role of women in society, captured the imaginations of many readers when it was first published. By reading it today, we can both enjoy a good story for its own sake and try to understand what compelled so many people to pay a hard-earned $1.50 for the chance to spend some time in the fictional world Ward created.
 We invite readers to discuss  Lady Rose’s Daughter on social media using the hashtag #LadyRoseLQ and to join our reading group on the online publishing platform Manifold, where you can use annotation and comment features to discuss the novel. To join us, you must first create an account  here by selecting “Need to sign up?” After creating an account you can connect to the discussion by following  this link. You can also  create your own reading group for your friends, book club, or class. (Details on how to do that can be found  here.)
 Over the next two weeks, we’ll publish a series of essays that challenge our common conceptions of popular literature by exploring diverse works from past best-seller lists—we’ll even delve into how those lists were compiled in the first place. These essays address topics from the first modern best seller in Russia to Edith Wharton’s disdain for best sellers, despite being a best-selling novelist herself. We’ll also publish an essay on the prehistory of romance novels and an examination of antislavery mass media, as well as infographics that explore the broader history of printing and book publishing. Our aim is to provide new insights into the cultures that produced and read popular works. Why have some books proved timeless while others seem to have resonated, albeit strongly, only with the readers of their moment?
 Please return to this page for updates about this series. If you have questions or comments, we’d love to hear from you at  editorial@laphamsquarterly.org.

 Essays 	•  The Best Seller Who Hated Best Sellers by Sheila Liming  •  Married to the Momism  by Emily Harnett
title: 被遗忘的畅销书

note:
圣地亚哥动漫展-就像2020年几乎所有大型会议、大会和聚会一样，由于持续的流行病，今年不得不转向仅限在线的虚拟形式。通常在SDCC这样的活动中有大量存在的媒体公司努力创造流媒体替代内容-但他们似乎忘记了告诉他们的版权机器人。
维亚康姆哥伦比亚广播公司(ViacomCBS)今天用一个小时的小组讨论拉开了序幕，展示了它目前和即将推出的一系列星际迷航项目：发现、皮卡德、下层甲板和奇怪的新世界。
评审团成员包括“探索”的演员和制片人，他们正在通读“探索”第二季大结局的第一幕“甜蜜的悲伤，第二部分”。增强的通读包括音效、特效镜头和故事板图像，这些都是为了支持演员们在客厅和家庭办公室里说台词。
尽管在家庭观众看来，演示文稿看起来不像是“探索”的真实剧集，但显然听起来足够接近了：在“星际迷航”宇宙虚拟面板开始之后，观众开始无法收看到视频。YouTube在视频的位置显示了一个内容ID警告：视频不可用：此视频包含来自CBS CID的内容，CBS CID已以版权为由将其屏蔽。&#34；视频不可用：此视频包含来自CBS CID的内容，CBS已以版权为由将其屏蔽。&#34；
在停电约20分钟后，面板恢复，虚拟面板的录制在回放中没有间隙。
显然，CBS不会从故意将自己的营销材料下线中受益；似乎最有可能的情况是，软件只是听到了匹配，进行了匹配，然后抛出了一个通知，直到有人可以覆盖和修复它。不幸的是，似乎各家媒体公司都不得不轮流急于纠正这个错误：两小时后，io9的记者贝丝·埃尔德金在推特上发帖称，由于卡通网络的母公司特纳提出版权要求，卡通网络的一个面板直播被下线。
note_en:
San Diego Comic-Con—like just about every large conference, convention, and gathering in 2020—has had to switch to an online-only, virtual format this year due to the continuing pandemic. Media companies that usually have a large presence at events like SDCC worked hard to create streaming alternative content—but it seems they forgot to tell their copyright bots.
 ViacomCBS kicked things off today with an  hour-long panel showing off its current slew of current and upcoming  Star Trek projects:   Discovery,   Picard,   Lower Decks, and   Strange New Worlds.
 The panel included the cast and producers of  Discovery doing a read-through of the first act of the season 2 finale, "Such Sweet Sorrow, Part 2." The "enhanced" read-through included sound effects, effects shots, and storyboard images meant to bolster the actors as they delivered lines from their living rooms and home offices.
 Even if the presentation didn't look like a real episode of  Discovery to the home viewer, it apparently sounded close enough: after the  Star Trek Universe virtual panel began viewers began to lose access to the stream. In place of the video, YouTube displayed a content ID warning reading: "Video unavailable: This video contains content from CBS CID, who has blocked it on copyright grounds."
 After being blacked out for about 20 minutes, the panel was restored, and the  recording of the virtual panel has no gaps in playback.
 Obviously, CBS would not benefit from pulling its own marketing material offline on purpose; it seems most likely that software simply heard a match, made a match, and threw up a notice until a human could override and fix it. Unfortunately, it seems an array of media companies are each going to have to rush to correct the error in turn: two hours later, io9 reporter Beth Elderkin  tweeted that a Cartoon Network panel livestream was pulled offline due to a copyright claim from Turner, Cartoon Network's parent company.
title: CBS过于热心的版权机器人击中了《星际迷航》虚拟动漫展
title_en: CBS’s overzealous copyright bots hit Star Trek virtual Comic-Con panel

周四，美国新冠肺炎病例超过400万例，因为大流行没有缓解的迹象。该国已有近14.4万人死于这种疾病。
根据COVID跟踪项目收集的数据，昨天记录了超过7万例新病例，7天内平均每天有近6.7万例新病例。
随着持续的激增，大流行期间的住院人数接近新的纪录。目前全国有59628人住院。这略低于4月15日创下的59,940点的前一峰值。死亡人数也在上升，七天平均每天有834人死亡。在过去的两天里，死亡人数已经超过了1000人。南部各州和西部各州的热点地区，如加利福尼亚州，正在见证最显著的疾病传播。
由于病人仍在涌入医院，专家预计死亡率在未来几周到几个月内将继续攀升。这就是一些进入医院的病人现在死于他们的疾病之前的时间。
雪上加霜的是，有报道称新冠肺炎检测实验室人满为患。将检测结果带回给患者的周转时间已经放缓到了缓慢的地步-在某些地方需要数周时间。这使得未感染的人在长时间的隔离中等待，这是不必要的。对于那些被感染的人来说，拖延已久的检测结果使得追踪接触者和隔离暴露的任何人在他们自己能够传播病毒之前是不可能的。
实验室可以使用每天可以进行数千次测试的高通量机器。事实上，美国现在进行的检测比大流行期间任何时候都多，周三报告了80万个检测结果。4月中旬，这个国家每天只进行大约15万次测试。
尽管如此，实验室可能会进行比现在更多的测试。正如美联社报道的那样，除了不断攀升的需求外，进行检测的实验室和医院还受到全球检测试剂和供应短缺的影响。例如，明尼苏达州罗切斯特市梅奥诊所的Bobbi Pritt告诉媒体，由于短缺，医院的机器仅以20%的容量运行。
美国最大的检测连锁店之一Quest Diagnostics告诉美联社，在目前病例激增的情况下，它根本跟不上需求。该公司表示，患者应该期待一周或更长时间的等待才能取得结果。它还建议医生推迟对低优先级患者进行测试，例如那些没有症状或已知与感染者有过接触的患者。
note_en:
The US surpassed 4 million cases of COVID-19 Thursday as the pandemic shows no signs of easing. Already, nearly 144,000 people in the country have died from the disease.
 Yesterday saw more than 70,000 new cases tallied, with a seven-day average of nearly 67,000 new cases per day, according to data collected by The COVID Tracking Project.
 With the ongoing surges, hospitalizations are nearing a new record during the pandemic. There are currently  59,628 people hospitalized across the country. That’s slightly below the previous peak on April 15 of 59,940. Deaths are also on the upswing, with a seven-day average of 834 deaths a day. The last two days have seen death tolls over 1,000. Southern states and hotspots in western states, such as California, are seeing the most significant disease spread.
 With the sick still streaming into hospitals, experts expect the death rate will continue to climb in the coming weeks to months. That’s how long it is before some of the patients entering the hospital now succumb to their disease.
  Exacerbating the crisis are reports that COVID-19 testing laboratories are overwhelmed. Turnaround time for getting test results back to patients has slowed to a crawl—in some place taking weeks. This has left the uninfected needlessly waiting in extended quarantines. For those who are infected, the long-delayed test results make it impossible to trace contacts and quarantine anyone exposed before they themselves could pass on the virus.
 Laboratories have access to high-throughput machines that can run thousands of tests a day. In fact, US is running more tests now than ever during the pandemic, reporting results 800,000 tests Wednesday. In mid-April, the country was running just around 150,000 tests a day.
 Still, labs could be running even more tests than they are now.  As the AP reports, laboratories and hospitals running tests are stifled by global shortages of testing reagents and supplies—in addition to the climbing demand. For instance, Bobbi Pritt of the Mayo Clinic in Rochester, Minnesota, told the outlet that the hospital’s machines are running at just 20 percent capacity due to shortages.
 Quest Diagnostics, one of the nation’s largest testing chains, told the AP that i t simply can’t keep up with demand during the current surge in cases. The company said patients should expect waits of a week or longer to get results. It also recommended that doctors hold off on testing low-priority patients, such as those without symptoms or known contact with an infected person.
title: 检测实验室遭受重创，住院人数飙升，新冠肺炎一路高歌猛进
title_en: Testing labs trounced, hospitalizations skyrocket as COVID-19 surges on

周三下午，众议员亚历山德里亚·奥卡西奥-科尔特斯(Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez)对一项综合性众议院拨款法案提出了一项修正案，该法案将禁止美军使用流行平台Twitch等视频游戏流媒体网站招募易受影响的年轻人，包括未成年儿童。
周三，奥卡西奥-科尔特斯告诉“主板”，陆军和海军通过直播平台招募易受影响的年轻人和儿童是令人难以置信的不负责任。
修正案草案禁止使用资金通过电子游戏和电子竞技平台招聘，可能不会进入最终法案。
众议院制定了一个版本的预算，在整个众议院投票表决之前，必须经过多个委员会的批准。那是在参议院介入之前。Twitch修正案可能会在前进的任何一步步履蹒跚，但奥卡西奥-科尔特斯提出修正案的事实完全说明，公众对军方使用视频游戏和Twitch作为招募工具的压力越来越大。
最近几周，军方利用ESPORTS征兵给五角大楼带来了一系列公关噩梦，从7月8日开始，活动家乔丹·乌尔(Jordan Uhl)因询问你最喜欢的美国国家是什么而被禁止进入陆军电子竞技Twitch频道。在聊天栏，然后链接到美国战争罪行的维基百科页面。
军方现在因为侵犯UL&#39；第一修正案的权利和至少300名其他类似被禁止的用户的言论自由而受到抨击。
骑士第一修正案研究所(Knight First Amendment Institute)周三在致征兵人员的一封信中表示，当政府有意向公众开放表达活动的空间时，它已经根据第一修正案创建了一个公共论坛，它不能基于观点在宪法上禁止演讲者进入该论坛。
乌尔说：看到国会女议员奥卡西奥-科尔特斯提出这项修正案，真是太棒了。我希望更多的人了解军队的征兵做法是多么危险和掠夺，特别是通过利用Twitch上的准社会关系。关闭这个工厂是拆除军工综合体所需的许多必要步骤之一。
奥卡西奥-科尔特斯说，使用电子游戏招募年轻人加入战争机器，对于五角大楼的任务来说是一种不可接受的方式-海军陆战队决定不参与这一平台就是明证。
战争不是一场游戏，她说，海军陆战队决定不参与这一招募工具应该是一个明确的信号，告诉军队的其他部门完全停止这种做法。&#34；她说，海军陆战队决定不参与这种招募工具的决定应该是一个明确的信号，让军队的其他部门完全停止这种做法。
note_en:
Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez on Wednesday afternoon introduced an amendment to an omnibus House Appropriations bill that would ban the U.S. military from using video game-streaming sites like popular platform Twitch to recruit impressionable young people, including underage children.
 "It's incredibly irresponsible for the Army and the Navy to be recruiting impressionable young people and children via live streaming platforms," Ocasio-Cortez  told  Motherboard Wednesday.
  The  draft amendment, which "prohibits the use of funds for recruiting via video gaming and e-sports platforms," may not make it to the final bill.
  The House writes one version of the budget that has to go through multiple committees before being voted on by the entire House. And that's before the Senate gets involved. The Twitch amendment could falter at any step along the way, but the fact that Ocasio-Cortez introduced the amendment at all speaks to the mounting public pressure against the military using video games and Twitch as a recruitment tool.
 The military's use of esports for recruitment has caused a number of public relations nightmares for the Pentagon in recent weeks, beginning on July 8 when activist Jordan Uhl was banned from the Army ESports Twitch channel for asking "what's your favorite u.s. w4r cr1me?" in the chat bar and then linking to the Wikipedia page of U.S. war crimes.
  The military is now under fire for violating Uhl's First Amendment rights and the freedom of speech rights of at least 300 other similarly banned users.
 "When the government intentionally opens a space to the public at large for expressive activity, it has created a 'public forum' under the First Amendment, and it cannot constitutionally bar speakers from that forum based on viewpoint," the Knight First Amendment Institute  said in a letter to military recruiters Wednesday.
  "It's fantastic to see Congresswoman Ocasio-Cortez introduce this amendment," said Uhl. "I'm hopeful more people understand how dangerous and predatory the military's recruiting practices are and especially by exploiting parasocial relationships on Twitch. Shutting this down is one of many necessary steps required to dismantle the military industrial complex."
 Using video games to recruit young people into the war machine, said Ocasio-Cortez, is an unacceptable approach to the Pentagon's mission—as evidenced by the Marines' decision not to engage in the platform.
 "War is not a game," she said, "and the Marine Corps' decision not to engage in this recruiting tool should be a clear signal to the other branches of the military to cease this practice entirely."
title: “战争不是游戏”：AOC将提交修正案，禁止美军在Twitch上征兵
title_en: 'War Is Not a Game': AOC to File Amendment Banning US Military From Recruiting on Twitch

据BuzzFeed新闻周四报道，Facebook的一名工程师在告别视频中表示，该公司在减轻伤害方面“失败”，并已“将这一失败写入我们的政策”。波士顿前工程师马克斯·王(Max Wang)在BuzzFeed获得的录音中声称，他于2011年加入Facebook，他表示，他不认为首席执行官马克·扎克伯格和其他公司领导人的行为是恶意的。
“但这并不意味着他们的行为不会伤害人，”王说。他补充说，他不相信Facebook“对使用我们平台的人的原始人类需求给予了足够的重视”。他说，该公司“受困于我们言论自由的意识形态”。
“我们在问‘为什么我们的政策允许这种事情？’”
王健林特别担心的是，该平台如何处理--或没有处理--特朗普总统发布的一篇有争议的帖子，他在帖子中评论说，“当抢劫开始，枪击就开始了”，这是关于明尼阿波利斯抗议者的。Facebook选择不采取任何行动，并将这篇帖子留在网上，尽管Twitter在推文版本中添加了一个标签，称其“美化暴力”。
王说，扎克伯格在一次公司会议上讨论这篇帖子时的评论感觉就像“煤气灯”。
“我们不是在问‘这件事遵循我们的政策吗？’我们在问‘为什么我们的政策允许这种事情？’“。王说。“为什么我们的政策不要求我们采取某种行动呢？”王补充说，扎克伯格的回应围绕着遵循政策，而不是固定政策。这一回应促使数十名Facebook员工在6月1日举行了虚拟罢工。
note_en:
A Facebook engineer said in a farewell video that the company was “failing” to mitigate harm and has “enshrined that failure in our policies,”   BuzzFeed News reported on Thursday. Max Wang, a Boston-based former engineer who claimed in the recording obtained by  BuzzFeed that he joined Facebook in 2011, said he didn’t think CEO Mark Zuckerberg and other company leaders were acting in malice.
 “But that does not mean their actions are not going to harm people,” Wang said. He added that he did not believe Facebook was “paying enough attention to the raw human needs of the people who use our platform.” The company is “trapped by our ideology of free expression,” he said.
 “We were asking ‘why do our policies allow for this thing?’”
 Of particular concern to Wang was how the platform handled — or didn’t handle— a controversial post by President Trump where he commented, “when the looting starts, the shooting starts,” about protesters in Minneapolis.  Facebook opted not to take any action and left the post up, despite Twitter adding a label to the tweeted version for “glorifying violence.”
 Wang said Zuckerberg’s comments at a company meeting discussing the post felt like “gaslighting.”
 “We weren’t asking ‘does this thing follow our policy?’ We were asking ‘why do our policies allow for this thing?’” Wang said. “Why don’t our policies require that we do take some kind of action?”  Zuckerberg framed his response around following policies rather than fixing policies, Wang added. The response prompted a  June 1st virtual walkout by dozens of Facebook employees.
title: 前Facebook工程师表示，该公司已将失败写入其政策
title_en: Former Facebook Engineer Says That the Company Has Enshrined Failure in Its Policies

note:
此外，人们对加州过山车般的冠状病毒经济感到愤怒、困惑和沮丧-在这种经济中，工作场所关闭又开又关，对那些仍然开放的工作场所的规定可能每天都会改变，而且执法往往看起来很松懈。
在感染和住院人数飙升的情况下，州长加文·纽瑟姆(Gavin Newsom)本月再次关闭了全州的一大批企业，包括餐厅、酒吧、电影院、扑克室、健身房、美发沙龙和一些办公室。
尽管如此，自3月份以来被暂时解雇或能够在家工作的数千名员工正被召回物理工作场所。
许多人，特别是那些得到强大工会支持的人，都在抵制。他们指出，过去四个月来，雇主未能防止新冠肺炎疫情爆发，即使是在医院、疗养院、快餐店、杂货店和仓库，在这些地方，工人被国家视为“必不可少”。
加州大学伯克利分校(UC Berkeley)劳工职业健康项目主任劳拉·斯托克(Laura Stock)表示：“从未离开工作场所的工人往往得不到足够的保护。”“现在很多人被迫回到他们觉得不安全的环境中工作。”
自3月以来，已有17,800多起工作场所对新冠肺炎的投诉涌入洛杉矶县公共卫生部。截至7月中旬，加州职业安全与健康部门(Cal/OSHA)已收到约3800起投诉。
斯托克说，企业往往不愿向工人透露他们是否接触过受感染的同事，县卫生部门和长期资金不足的加州/OSHA之间的管辖权尚不清楚。
被召回工作场所的休假员工如果不回来，通常会失去失业救济金。“这是一个可怕的情况，”斯托克说。“人们必须在薪水和健康之间做出选择--不仅是他们自己的健康，还有他们家庭和社区的健康。”
本月，在洛杉矶市中心菲格罗亚街(Figueroa Street)的一个角落，数十名蒙面管家和洗碗工举行了午餐时间的集会，挥舞着手写的标语，上面写着“我没有安全感”和“暂停酒店重新开业”。
自6月12日洛杉矶县允许酒店重新向公众开放以来，代表南加州约2.5万名酒店从业人员的工会Unite Here Local 11就发起了抗议活动。自3月以来，根据该县的一项计划，酒店只被允许为急救人员、被隔离的必要工作人员和一些无家可归者提供服务。
布兰卡·格雷罗(Blanca Guerrero)最近被召回JW万豪酒店(JW Marriott Santa Monica Le Merigot)的客房管理工作，她说，她的老板没有提供如何在不冒着被其他工人和客人曝光的风险进行清洁的培训。
两周前，一位客人不戴面罩走近她，在她的清洁车上咳嗽，但当她告诉她的主管时，她说，他们什么也没做。她说，当一名同事生病时，酒店拒绝支付新冠肺炎的检查费用。
万豪的一位女发言人拒绝对格雷罗的情况发表评论，但在一份声明中表示：“我们客人和同事的福祉是至高无上的。万豪有完善的清洁流程和培训。“。
尽管48岁的格雷罗称自己“很害怕”抓到新冠肺炎，但她说，她不能辞职，因为她的最低工资可以养活她19岁和23岁的两个女儿。
当地11联席主席库尔特·彼得森(Kurt Petersen)说，Unite Here已经向洛杉矶县卫生局提交了12起投诉，记录了85起病毒感染事件。他说：“我们都想重返工作岗位，但我只是认为我们还没有准备好。”他补充说，尽管安装了有机玻璃护栏并提供了防护设备，但酒店并没有雇佣足够的员工来确保彻底清洁。
6月下旬对当地11名成员的调查显示，75%的人不想在大流行过去之前被迫重返工作岗位。工会已向洛杉矶县监事会请愿，要求暂停重新开放旅游和休闲旅行酒店，直到确保更严格的安全协议、工人培训和接触者追踪。
由于洛杉矶县6月份的失业率为19.5%，随着企业再次关门，失业率可能会上升，阻碍旅游业可能会延长已经对当地经济造成的巨大打击。在新冠肺炎遇袭之前，预计2020年将有5,100万游客。现在的预测是大约有3000万游客。据洛杉矶旅游和会议委员会(Los Angeles Travel And Conference Board)称，这一下降可能意味着支出减少110亿美元。
在距离酒店工作人员发起抗议的地方几个街区远的克拉拉·肖特里奇·福尔茨刑事司法中心，律师威廉·海耶斯和其他数十名副公设辩护律师被归类为基本工人。他在大流行期间一直在工作，自3月份陪审团审判暂停以来，他主要通过视频与被监禁的客户联系。
但在7月6日，这座庞大的建筑重新向公众开放。当37岁的海耶斯乘坐电梯往返于他19楼的办公室和其他楼层的法庭之间时，人群现在正在转来转去。陪审团审判将于8月或9月恢复。
“和我一起工作的人都很害怕，”他说。“就在住院人数和死亡人数激增的时候，我们却在敞开心扉，这是一种疯狂。”
他说，监狱里的检测“表明，和其他地方一样，病毒在不断上升”，尽管每天都有数百名被告被带上法庭。
在法庭上，他说，“社会距离不是强制的。七八个人跳进电梯。市民和法院人员不是不戴口罩，就是下巴下面戴口罩，要不就是一只耳朵挂着。“。
海耶斯说，虽然一些法官对口罩持“好战态度”，但“仍有法官在法官席上不戴口罩。”
他说，如果海耶斯不再来工作，他不仅会没收工资，还会让等待出庭的客户失望。他说，他们在监狱里逗留，通过冠状病毒“可能会被判处死刑”。
海耶斯说：“我和我的同事，其中一些是老年人，愿意冒这个险。”“但他们对我们的要求太高了。”
洛杉矶县高等法院的一名女发言人拒绝直接回应海耶斯关于执法的断言，但在一封电子邮件中表示，法官在5月份被指示戴面罩。7月6日的一项命令要求在所有县法院覆盖面部，保持社交距离，并使用洗手液。
“法院已经遵守了所有州和县的公共卫生要求，”她写道。“警长的工作人员负责执行社交疏远协议。”她写道，高人流量区域每天至少清理两次，而地板、长凳、法庭和电梯都有物理距离的标记。
南加州各地的数十名工人-包括餐厅、杂货店、呼叫中心、航空航天公司、服装厂和隔间密密麻麻的办公室-都联系了时报，描述了不安全的工作场所。大多数情况下，他们要求匿名，因为他们担心丢掉工作。最严重的疫情之一：服装制造商洛杉矶服装公司(Los Angeles Apparel)的300多名工人检测出病毒呈阳性，已有4人死亡。
通过集体谈判保护成员免受报复的有组织劳工，正在领导公众抵制重返工作岗位的倡议，并提出更严格的安全措施的迫切要求。
6月中旬，代表大约1.7万名迪士尼度假区员工的12个工会写信给纽瑟姆，称如期于7月17日开放迪士尼乐园是不安全的。这封信发出后不到一周，由于南加州冠状病毒感染激增，沃尔特·迪士尼公司(Walt Disney Co.)宣布，迪士尼乐园本月将不会重新开放。它还没有确定新的日期。
工会发言人奥斯汀·林奇(Austin Lynch)说，该公司“过于自信，没有意识到开设一家可能会导致人死亡的企业意味着什么。”
迪士尼为其重新开放计划辩护，理由是加强了清洁，限制了容量，并在公园入口处测量了客人的温度。但工会表示，在病毒得到控制之前，一个有人群和游乐设施的主题公园不应该开放，因为执行物理距离和面具规则太难了。
23岁的乔伊·滨本(Joey Hamamoto)是迪士尼大加州酒店的停车场服务员，在3月份被暂时解雇之前，他的工资是最低工资，外加小费。他说，如果迪士尼不符合工会的条件，他将不愿回来，因为“我会感到没有安全感”。
滨本的四个儿子中有一个患有哮喘，他的哥哥最近感染了新冠肺炎，他的父亲在5月份中风。他担心把病毒带回家。
滨本说，代客休息室有壁橱那么大，爬进爬出多辆车是有风险的：世界卫生组织(World Health Organization)最近承认，冠状病毒可能会在封闭空间的空气中徘徊。“我们这些在酒店工作的人是客人最先看到的人，来自世界各地的人，”他说。“很难说谁能携带什么东西。”
公共雇员工会也同样强调这一点。7月10日，代表3.2万名教师、辅导员、护士和图书馆员的洛杉矶联合教师组织(United Teachers Los Angeles)发布了对其成员进行的民意调查结果，结果显示83%的人反对重新开放校园。
三天后，在新冠肺炎案件激增的情况下，洛杉矶学区宣布，在另行通知之前，将不会重新开放面授课程。中学西班牙语教师尼克·哈里斯(Nick Harris)说：“你不能告诉一个人，你的孩子(应该)上学来拯救这个经济--也许你的孩子会死。”
周五，纽瑟姆取消了该州新冠肺炎观察名单上32个县的面对面课程。这对克里斯汀·佩卡姆(Kristen Peckham)来说是一个小小的安慰，她在三一县农村的幼儿园任教，那里不在名单上。
佩卡姆的学校，烧伤牧场小学，将不得不坚持严格的标准，包括温度测试，洗手和6英尺的距离。但三年级以下的学生不会被要求戴口罩。
她承认，远程教学一直是一个挑战。有些学生上网不可靠。孩子们只有5岁和6岁，独立学习几乎是不可能的。
她所在学校的其他老师使用Zoom直播课堂，但Peckham想出了其他方法。她录制了课程视频，方便时供学生家长下载，然后让自己回答问题。她还寄出了一包包的作业，让学生手工完成，这带来了更好的参与度。
佩克汉姆说，她愿意继续这样做-但她不愿冒着在工作中抓到新冠肺炎的风险，可能会传染给她的丈夫。
她已经告诉她的校长她不会再回教室了。“我不能冒与孩子们面对面的机会。”
佩卡姆并不孤单。数十万加州工人患有潜在的健康状况，如糖尿病和自身免疫性疾病，这可能会加剧冠状病毒感染。
洛杉矶皮科-罗伯逊(Pico-Robertson)附近的一家零售商Feldmar Watch Co.销售1000美元的钟表，该公司已经召回了几名员工，只限预约销售。在这家商店工作了14年的洛雷尔·门罗伊(Lorel Monroy)不在志愿者之列。
“我不想处理库存，然后把它放在客户的手腕上，”她说。“如果我感染了，我很可能不能靠呼吸机生存了。”
此外，由于学校停课，她的丈夫在做卡车运输工作，她不愿把15岁的儿子一个人留在家里。“他是一个负责任的孩子，”她说。“但是青少年可能会做出疯狂的事情。”
到目前为止，门罗伊的老板是理解的。但她说，如果她被召回并失去领取失业救济金的资格，她可能不得不返回，否则就会冒着失去南洛杉矶的家丧失抵押品赎回权的风险。
经济的下滑状况给她带来了压力，因为许多公司的休假变成了永久性的裁员。“一想到我可能会被召回，我就很害怕，”她说。“但如果没人给我回电话，我就更害怕了。”
note_en:
Also anger, confusion and frustration with California’s roller-coaster coronavirus economy — in which workplaces close and open and close again, rules for those that remain open can change by the day, and enforcement often seems lax.
 Amid soaring infections and hospitalizations, Gov. Gavin Newsom this month again  shut down a large swath of businesses across the state, including dine-in restaurants, bars, movie theaters, card rooms, gyms, hair salons and some offices.
 Nonetheless, thousands of employees who have been furloughed or able to work from home since March are being called back to physical workplaces.
    Many, especially those backed by powerful labor unions, are resisting. They cite the failure of employers over the last four months to prevent COVID-19 outbreaks, even in  hospitals,  nursing homes,  fast-food outlets,  grocery stores and  warehouses where workers were deemed “essential” by the state.
  “Workers who never left the workplace were often not sufficiently protected,” said Laura Stock, director of the Labor Occupational Health Program at UC Berkeley. “Now a lot of people have been forced to go back to work in circumstances they don’t feel are safe.”
 Since March, more than 17,800 workplace complaints about COVID-19 have poured into the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health. California’s Division of Occupational Safety and Health, known as Cal/OSHA, had received  some 3,800 complaints as of mid-July.
  Businesses are often less than forthcoming with workers about whether they have been exposed to an infected colleague, Stock said, and jurisdiction between county health departments and Cal/OSHA, which has long been underfunded, is unclear.
 Furloughed employees called back to the workplace usually lose unemployment benefits if they don’t return. “It’s a terrible situation,” Stock said. “People have to choose between a paycheck and their health — not only their own health, but their health of their family and their community.”
 On a corner of Figueroa Street in downtown Los Angeles this month, dozens of masked housekeepers and dishwashers held a lunchtime rally, waving hand-lettered signs reading, “I don’t feel safe” and “Pause reopening of hotels.”
   Unite Here Local 11, a union that represents about 25,000 hospitality workers in Southern California, has mounted protests ever since Los Angeles County allowed hotels to reopen to the general public June 12. Since March, hotels had been permitted to serve only first responders, essential workers in quarantine and some homeless people under a county program.
 Blanca Guerrero, recently called back to her housekeeping job at the JW Marriott Santa Monica Le Merigot, said her bosses have not offered training on how to clean without risking exposure from other workers and guests.
 Two weeks ago, a guest approached her without a face covering and coughed on her cleaning cart, but when she told her supervisors, she said, they did nothing. When a co-worker fell ill, she said, the hotel refused to pay for a COVID-19 test.
 A Marriott spokeswoman declined to comment on Guerrero’s situation but said in a statement: “The well-being of our guests and associates is of paramount importance. Marriott has well-established cleaning processes and training in place.”
  Despite what Guerrero, 48, called her “big fear” of catching COVID-19, she said, she can’t quit because her minimum-wage pay helps support her two daughters, ages 19 and 23.
 Unite Here has filed a dozen complaints with the L.A. County health department, documenting 85 virus infections, said Kurt Petersen, co-president of Local 11. “We all want to go back to work, but I just don’t think we are ready yet,” he said, adding that despite installing plexiglass barriers and providing protective equipment, hotels are not hiring sufficient staff to ensure thorough cleaning.
 A survey of Local 11 members in late June showed 75% didn’t want to be forced back to work before the pandemic had passed. The union has  petitioned the L.A. County Board of Supervisors to pause the reopening of hotels for tourism and leisure travel until stricter safety protocols, worker training and contact tracing can be ensured.
 With Los Angeles County’s unemployment rate at 19.5% in June, and probably rising as businesses shut again, discouraging tourism could prolong the already massive hit to the local economy. Before COVID-19 struck, 51 million tourists were expected in 2020. Now the forecast is for about 30 million visitors. The decline could mean an $11-billion drop in spending, according to the Los Angeles Tourism and Convention Board.
  At the Clara Shortridge Foltz Criminal Justice Center a few blocks from where the hotel workers launched their protest, attorney William Hayes is classified as an essential worker — along with scores of other deputy public defenders. He has worked through the pandemic, connecting with jailed clients mainly via video since jury trials were suspended in March.
 But on July 6, the hulking building reopened to the public. Crowds are now milling about as Hayes, 37, rides the elevators between his 19th-floor office and courtrooms on other floors. Jury trials are set to resume in August or September.
  “People I work with are frightened,” he said. “It’s a kind of madness that we’re opening up just as hospitalizations and deaths are spiking.”
  Testing in the jails “shows, like everywhere else, the virus is going up and up and up,” he said, even as hundreds of defendants are brought to court every day.
 At the court, he said, “social distancing is not enforced. Seven or eight people jump into elevators. Members of the public and court personnel are either not wearing masks or wearing them under their chins, or hanging off one ear.”
 While some judges are “militant” about masks, Hayes said, “there are still judges who do not wear masks on the bench.”
 If Hayes were to stop coming to work, he said, he would not only forfeit his salary but also let down clients waiting for their day in court. “They’re exposed to a potential death sentence” via the coronavirus by lingering in jail, he said.
  “My colleagues and I, some of them elderly, are willing to take the risk,” Hayes said. “But they’re asking way too much of us.”
 A spokeswoman for the L.A. County Superior Court declined to directly address Hayes’ assertions about enforcement but said in an email that judges were directed in May to wear face coverings. And a  July 6 order mandated facial coverage, social distancing and the use of hand sanitizer in all county courthouses.
 “The court has complied with all state and county public health requirements,” she wrote. “The sheriff’s staff is tasked with enforcing the social distancing protocol.” High-traffic areas are cleaned at least twice a day while floors, benches, courtrooms and elevators have physical-distancing markings, she wrote.
 Scores of workers across Southern California — including those at restaurants, grocery stores, call centers, aerospace firms, garment factories and cubicle-filled offices — have  contacted The Times to describe unsafe workplaces. Mostly, they requested anonymity for fear of losing their jobs. Among the most severe outbreaks: More than 300 workers at garment manufacturer Los Angeles Apparel tested positive for the virus, and  four have died.
    Organized labor, which protects members from retaliation through collective bargaining, is leading public resistance to back-to-work initiatives and  airing urgent demands for stricter safety measures.
 In mid-June, a dozen unions representing about 17,000 Disneyland Resort employees  wrote to Newsom to say it would be unsafe to open the park as scheduled July 17. Less than a week after the letter was sent, amid a surge in Southern California coronavirus infections, Walt Disney Co. announced the park  would not reopen this month. It has yet to set a new date.
 The company was “overconfident and not realizing what it means to open a business where people could die,” said Austin Lynch, a union spokesman.
    Disney defends its reopening plan, citing enhanced cleaning, limits on capacity and the taking of guests’ temperatures at the park entrance. But the unions say a theme park with crowds and rides should not open until the virus is under control, because enforcing physical distancing and mask rules would be too difficult.
 Joey Hamamoto, 23, a parking attendant at Disney’s Grand Californian Hotel, earned minimum wage plus tips before he was furloughed in March. If Disney doesn’t meet the union’s terms, he would be reluctant to return because “I won’t feel safe,” he said.
 One of Hamamoto’s four sons has asthma, his brother recently contracted COVID-19, and his father had a stroke in May. He worries about bringing the virus home.
  The valet break room is the size of a closet, Hamamoto said, and climbing in and out of multiple cars is risky: The World Health Organization recently acknowledged the  coronavirus can linger in the air in enclosed spaces. ”We who work in the hotel are the first ones the guests see, people from all over the world,” he said. “There is no telling who can be carrying what.”
 Public employee unions are equally emphatic. On July 10, United Teachers Los Angeles, which represents 32,000 teachers, counselors, nurses and librarians, issued the results of a poll of its members showing 83% opposed to reopening campuses.
 Three days later, amid a surge in COVID-19 cases, the L.A. school district announced it would not reopen for in-person classes until further notice. “You cannot tell a person your child [should] go to school to save this economy — and maybe your kid will die,” said Nick Harris, a middle school Spanish teacher.
   On Friday,  Newsom canceled in-person classes in 32 counties on the state’s COVID-19 watchlist. That was small comfort to Kristen Peckham, who teaches kindergarten in rural Trinity County, which is not on the list.
 Peckham’s school, Burnt Ranch Elementary, will have to adhere to strict standards, including temperature tests, hand-washing and six feet of distancing. But students below third grade won’t be required to wear masks.
  Remote teaching has been a challenge, she acknowledges. Some students don’t have dependable access to the internet. And the kids are 5 and 6 years old, making independent learning near impossible.
  Other teachers at her school used Zoom to livestream classes, but Peckham came up with other ways. She had recorded videos of lessons for her students’ parents to download when convenient, then made herself available to answer questions. She also sent packets of work for students to complete by hand, which yielded even better participation.
 Peckham said she’s willing to keep doing that — but she isn’t willing to risk catching COVID-19 on the job and possibly infecting her husband.
 She has told her principal she won’t return to a classroom. “I could not take the chance of being face to face with the kids.”
 Peckham is not alone. Hundreds of thousands of California workers have underlying health conditions, such as diabetes and autoimmune diseases, that could aggravate a coronavirus infection.
  Feldmar Watch Co., a retailer in L.A.'s Pico-Robertson neighborhood that sells thousand-dollar timepieces, has called back several staffers for appointment-only sales. Lorel Monroy, who has worked at the store for 14 years, was not among the volunteers.
  “I don’t want to handle inventory and put it on a client’s wrist,” she said. “If I got infected, I probably wouldn’t survive on a ventilator.”
 Moreover, with schools closed and her husband at his trucking job, she is reluctant to leave her 15-year-old son home alone. “He’s a responsible kid,” she said. “But teenagers can do crazy stuff.”
  So far, Monroy’s boss is understanding. But if she gets called back and loses eligibility for unemployment benefits, she said, she probably would have to return or risk losing her South Los Angeles home to foreclosure.
 The sinking state of the economy weighs on her, as furloughs at many companies turn into permanent layoffs. “It is scary to think I might be called back,” she said. “But if I don’t get called back, I’m more scared.”
title: 工人们害怕重返工作岗位。许多人抵制这一呼吁
title_en: Workers fear returning to work. Many are resisting the call

note:
一项新的加托全国调查发现，美国的自我审查(Self-​)审查制度正在上升。近三分之二(62%)的美国人表示，如今的政治气候阻止他们说出他们相信的话，因为其他人可能会觉得这些话令人反感。自2017年58%的美国人同意这一说法以来，自我​审查的美国人比例上升了几个百分点。
这些担忧跨越了党派界限。大多数民主党人(52%)、无党派人士(59%)和共和党人(77%)都同意他们有不敢分享的政治观点。
然而，强大的自由派脱颖而出，成为唯一觉得自己可以表达自己的政治团体。近60%(58%)的坚定自由派人士认为他们可以说出自己的信仰。然而，中间派自由派有不同的感受。微弱多数(52%)的自由派认为他们必须自我​审查，64%的温和派和77%的保守派也是如此。这表明，政治表达是民主党联盟在中间派民主党人和他们的左翼之间划分的一个问题。
有什么改变了吗？2017年，大多数中间派自由派人士对他们能够表达自己的观点充满信心(54%)。然而，今天，略低于一半(48%)的人有同样的感受。感觉不能开放的占比从2017年的45%增加到今天的52%，提高了7个百分点。事实上，已经出现了全面的转变，在所有政治团体中，越来越多的人感到如履薄冰。
虽然强大的自由主义者是唯一觉得自己可以说出自己信仰的群体，但感到有压力进行自我​审查的比例从2017年的30%上升到2020年的42%，上升了12个百分点。自我​审查的温和派占比上升7个百分点，从57%升至64%，保守派占比上升70-77个百分点，也是7-​个百分点的升幅。坚定的保守派是唯一几乎没有变化的群体。他们现在(77%)表示保留观点的可能性与2017年(76%)差不多。
自营(Self-​)审查在人口统计群体中也很普遍。​近三分之二的拉美裔美国人(65%)和白人美国人(64%)和近一半的非裔美国人(49%)有他们不敢分享的政治观点。大多数男性(65%)和女性(59%)，收入超过100,000美元的人(60%)和收入低于20,000美元的人(58%)，35岁以下和65岁以上的人(55%)和65岁以上的人(66%)，宗教人士(71%)和非​宗教人士(56%)都认为政治气候阻碍了他们表达真正的信仰。
50%的强大自由派支持解雇特朗普的捐赠者；36%的强大保守派支持解雇拜登的捐赠者。
调查发现，许多美国人认为个人的私人政治捐款应该会影响他们的就业。近四分之一(22%)的美国人会支持解雇一位亲自向民主党总统候选人乔·拜登的竞选活动捐款的美国企业高管。更有31%的人支持解雇一名为特朗普连任​竞选活动捐款的美国企业高管。
政治子群体的支持率上升。支持解雇亲自向特朗普捐款的高管的强大自由派人士的支持率上升到50%。超过三分之一(36%)的强烈保守派人士支持解雇一名向拜登总统竞选捐款的高管。
与年长的美国人相比，美国年轻人也更有可能支持惩罚工作中个人向特朗普捐款的人。在30岁以下的美国人中，44%(44%)的​支持解雇高管，如果他们向特朗普捐款的话。在55岁以上的人群中，这一比例下降到22%-相差20个​点。拜登捐赠者的年龄差距也存在，但不那么明显。在30岁以下的美国人中，有27%(27%)的人支持解雇为拜登捐款的高管，而55岁以上的人中这一比例为20%--相差7个百分点(​​)。
近三分之一(32%)的有工作的美国人表示，他们个人担心，如果他们的政治观点被曝光，他们会错失职业机会或失去工作。这些结果尤其值得注意，因为大多数个人对政治候选人的竞选捐款都是众所周知的，很容易在网上找到。
这不仅仅是政治光谱的一个方面：31%的自由派、30%的温和派和34%的保守派担心他们的政治观点可能会让他们被解雇或损害他们的职业生涯。这表明，不一定只有一组特定的观点已经超越了可接受的公共话语。取而代之的是，这些结果与“如履薄冰”的命题更加一致，人们越来越担心广泛的政治观点可能会冒犯他人，或者可能会对自己产生负面影响。
这些担忧也是跨党派的，尽管更多的共和党人担心：28%的民主党人，31%的无党派人士和38%的共和党人担心他们的政治观点会如何影响他们的职业轨迹。
不同背景的美国人都有这样的担忧，即如果他们的政治观点被发现，他们的就业可能会受到不利影响：38%的西班牙裔美国人，22%的非洲裔美国人，31%的白人美国人，35%的男性，27%的女性，36%的年收入低于2万美元的家庭，以及33%的年收入超过10万美元的家庭。
一些人更担心因为政治观点而失去工作或错失就业机会。那些受教育程度最高的人最受关注。几乎一半(44%)拥有​研究生学位的美国人表示，他们担心如果别人发现他们的政治观点，他们的职业生涯可能会受到损害。相比之下，34%的大学毕业生、28%的有一定大学经验的人和25%的高中毕业生表示，他们担心自己的职业生涯可能会受到损害，相比之下，这一比例分别为34%、28%和25%。
但这种教育鸿沟似乎在很大程度上是由党派之争推动的。拥有研究生学位的民主党人(25%)和高中毕业生(23%)一样，担心他们的政治观点可能会损害他们的就业。然而，在上大学和研究生院的共和党人中发生了一个非常重大的转变。大约四分之一拥有高中学位的共和党人(27%)或一些大学共和党人(26%)担心他们的政治观点可能会在工作中伤害他们-但在共和党大学毕业生中，这一数字上升到40%，在拥有​研究生学位的人中，这一数字上升到60%。在独立人士中也观察到了类似的趋势。有这些顾虑的独立人士的比例从高中毕业生中的18%上升到部分大学毕业生中的35%、大学毕业生中的41%和​后毕业生中的49%。
无论政治观点如何，年轻人也比老年人更关心。调查了所有65岁以下的美国人，30岁以下的人中有37%担心他们的政治观点可能会损害他们的职业轨迹，相比之下，30-54岁的​人群和55-64岁的​人群中分别有30%和24%的人担心他们的政治观点会损害他们的职业轨迹。但考虑到政治观点，年龄差距更显着。在30岁以下的共和党人中，微弱多数(51%)担心他们的观点可能会损害他们的职业前景，相比之下，30-44岁的​人群中有39%，45-54岁的​人群中有34%，55-64岁的​人群中有28%。民主党人反映了一个类似但不那么明显的模式。30岁以下的民主党人中有三分之一(33%)担心他们的观点可能会损害他们目前和未来的工作，相比之下，30-54岁的​年龄段的民主党人中这一比例为27%，55-64岁的​年龄段的民主党人中这一比例为19%。
这些数据表明，来自所有政治信仰和背景的极大少数美国人-特别是在美国大学度过更多时间的年轻人-最有可能隐藏自己的观点，因为他们担心受到经济处罚。
一个特别令人惊讶的发现是，有这些担忧的美国人在某种程度上更有可能支持解雇拜登或特朗普的捐赠者。在那些担心自己的政治观点可能会损害他们的就业的人中，三分之一(33%)的人支持解雇拜登或特朗普的捐赠者，相比之下，在那些不担心他们的观点会影响他们的工作的人中，这一比例为24%。这表明，那些担心因其政治观点而受到报复或经济惩罚的人，与那些为其他人寻求同样想法的人并不完全不同。
将这些结果放在一起表明，拥有不同政治观点和背景的绝大多数美国人自我审查(Self-​)自己的政治观点。来自不同人口群体的大量数据表明，在被社会边缘化的过程中，被隐瞒的观点可能不仅仅是激进或边缘的观点。相反，这些观点中的许多可能会被大量的人所认同。如此广泛的共识可能会影响人们对突出政策问题的看法，并最终影响他们的投票方式。但是，如果人们觉得不能讨论这些重要的政策问题，这些观点就没有机会被审查、理解和改革。
卡托研究所2020年夏季全国调查是由卡托研究所与YouGov合作设计和进行的。YouGov在2020年7月1日至6日期间在网上收集了2000名18岁及以上的美国人的全国样本。设置了限制，以确保只有YouGov选择并联系的人才能参与。在95%的置信水平下，调查的误差幅度为+/-2.36个百分点。
note_en:
A new Cato national survey finds that self‐​censorship is on the rise in the United States. Nearly two-thirds—62%—of Americans say the political climate these days prevents them from saying things they believe because others might find them offensive. The share of Americans who self‐​censor has risen several points  since 2017 when 58% of Americans agreed with this statement.
    These fears cross partisan lines. Majorities of Democrats (52%), independents (59%) and Republicans (77%) all agree they have political opinions they are afraid to share.
      Strong liberals stand out, however, as the only political group who feel they can express themselves. Nearly 6 in 10 (58%) of staunch liberals feel they can say what they believe. However, centrist liberals feel differently. A slim majority (52%) of liberals feel they have to self‐​censor, as do 64% of moderates, and 77% of conservatives. This demonstrates that political expression is an issue that divides the Democratic coalition between centrist Democrats and their left flank.
    What’s changed? In 2017 most centrist liberals felt confident (54%) they could express their views. However today, slightly less than half (48%) feel the same. The share who feel they cannot be open increased 7 points from 45% in 2017 to 52% today. In fact, there have been shifts across the board, where more people among all political groups feel they are walking on eggshells.
    Although strong liberals are the only group who feel they can say what they believe, the share who feel pressured to self‐​censor rose 12 points from 30% in 2017 to 42% in 2020. The share of moderates who self‐​censor increased 7 points from 57% to 64%, and the share of conservatives rose 70% to 77%, also a 7‐​point increase. Strong conservatives are the only group with little change. They are about as likely now (77%) to say they hold back their views as in 2017 (76%).
 Self‐​censorship is widespread across demographic groups as well. Nearly two‐​thirds of Latino Americans (65%) and White Americans (64%) and nearly half of African Americans (49%) have political views they are afraid to share. Majorities of men (65%) and women (59%), people with incomes over $100,000 (60%) and people with incomes less than $20,000 (58%), people under 35 (55%) and over 65 (66%), religious (71%) and non‐​religious (56%) all agree that the political climate prevents them from expressing their true beliefs.
  50% of Strong Liberals Support Firing Trump Donors; 36% of Strong Conservatives Support Firing Biden Donors
  The survey found that many Americans think a person’s private political donations should impact their employment. Nearly a quarter (22%) of Americans would support firing a business executive who personally donates to Democratic presidential candidate Joe Biden’s campaign. Even more, 31% support firing a business executive who donates to Donald Trump’s re‐​election campaign.
 Support rises among political subgroups. Support increases to 50% of strong liberals who support firing executives who personally donate to Trump. And more than a third (36%) of strong conservatives support firing an executive for donating to Biden’s presidential campaign.
 Young Americans are also more likely than older Americans to support punishing people at work for personal donations to Trump. Forty‐​four percent (44%) of Americans under 30 support firing executives if they donate to Trump. This share declines to 22% among those over 55 years old—a 20‐​point difference. An age gap also exists for Biden donors, but is less pronounced. Twenty‐​seven percent (27%) of Americans under 30 support firing executives who donate to Biden compared to 20% of those over 55—a 7‐​point difference.
        Nearly a third (32%) of employed Americans say they personally are worried about missing out on career opportunities or losing their job if their political opinions became known. These results are particularly notable given that most personal campaign contributions to political candidates are public knowledge and can easily be found online.
 And it’s not just one side of the political spectrum: 31% of liberals, 30% of moderates and 34% of conservatives are worried their political views could get them fired or harm their career trajectory. This suggests that it’s not necessarily just one particular set of views that has moved outside of acceptable public discourse. Instead these results are more consistent with a “walking on eggshells” thesis that people increasingly fear a wide range of political views could offend others or could negatively impact themselves.
 These concerns are also cross‐​partisan, although more Republicans are worried: 28% of Democrats, 31% of independents, and 38% of Republicans are worried about how their political opinions could impact their career trajectories.
 Americans with diverse backgrounds share this concern that their employment could be adversely affected if their political views were discovered: 38% of Hispanic Americans, 22% of African Americans, 31% of White Americans, 35% of men, 27% of women, 36% of households earning less than $20,000 a year, and 33% of households earning more than $100,000 a year agree.
 Some are more worried about losing their jobs or missing out on job opportunities because of political views. Those with the highest levels of education are most concerned. Almost half (44%) of Americans with post‐​graduate degrees say they are worried their careers could be harmed if others discovered their political opinions, compared to 34% of college graduates, 28% of those with some college experience, and 25% of high school graduates
    But this educational divide appears largely driven by partisanship. Democrats with graduate degrees (25%) are about as likely as high school graduates (23%) to be worried their political views could harm their employment. However, a major shift occurs among Republicans who attend college and graduate school. About a quarter of Republicans with high school degrees (27%) or some college (26%) worry their political opinions could harm them at work—but this number increases to 40% among Republican college graduates and 60% of those with post‐​graduate degrees. A similar trend is observed among independents. The share of independents who have these concerns increases from 18% among high school graduates, to 35% among those with some college, 41% of college graduates, and 49% of post‐​graduates.
 Younger people are also more concerned than older people, irrespective of political viewpoint. Examining all Americans under 65, 37% of those under 30 are worried their political opinions could harm their career trajectories, compared to 30% of 30–54 year‐​olds and 24% of 55–64 year‐​olds. But the age gap is more striking taking into account political views. A slim majority (51%) of Republicans under 30 fear their views could harm their career prospects compared to 39% of 30–44 year‐​olds, 34% of 45–54 year‐​olds, and 28% of 55–64 year‐​old Republicans. Democrats reflect a similar but less pronounced pattern. A third (33%) of Democrats under 30 worry they have views that could harm their current and future jobs, compared to 27% of 30–54 year‐​olds, and 19% of 55–64 year‐​old Democrats.
 These data suggest that a significant minority of Americans from all political persuasions and backgrounds—particularly younger people who have spent more time in America’s universities—are most likely to hide their views for fear of financial penalty.
 A particularly surprising finding was that Americans who have these concerns are somewhat more likely to support the firing of Biden or Trump donors. A third (33%) among those who worry that their political views could harm their employment supported firing either Biden or Trump donors, compared to 24% of those who were not worried about their views impacting their jobs. This suggests that those who fear reprisal or economic penalty for their political views are not entirely distinct from those who seek the same for others.
    Taking these results together indicates that a significant majority of Americans with diverse political views and backgrounds self‐​censor their political opinions. This large number from across demographic groups suggests withheld opinions may not simply be radical or fringe perspectives in the process of being socially marginalized. Instead many of these opinions may be shared by a large number of people. Opinions so widely shared are likely shaping how people think about salient policy issues and ultimately impacting how they vote. But if people feel they cannot discuss these important policy matters, such views will not have an opportunity to be scrutinized, understood, or reformed.
  The Cato Institute Summer 2020 National Survey was designed and conducted by the Cato Institute in collaboration with YouGov. YouGov collected responses online during July 1–6, 2020 from a national sample of 2,000 Americans 18 years of age and older. Restrictions are put in place to ensure that only the people selected and contacted by YouGov are allowed to participate. The margin of error for the survey is +/- 2.36 percentage points at the 95% level of confidence.
title: 62%的美国人说他们有不敢分享的政治观点
title_en: 62% of Americans Say They Have Political Views They’re Afraid to Share

note:
路透纽约8月23日电-随着企业允许更多员工在家工作，美国企业正在缩减房地产足迹，这对传统写字楼所有者的底线构成越来越大的威胁，也表明企业正在寻找因冠状病毒大流行而削减成本的方法.。
路透社(Reuters)对过去一周季度财报电话会议的分析显示，超过25家大公司计划在未来一年减少办公空间，此举旨在减少仅次于企业薪资的第二大支出。
能源公司哈里伯顿公司(Halliburton Co)表示，打算关闭100多个设施。金融服务公司道富银行表示，在增加额外空间之前，该公司将把分配到一个办公室的员工增加近一倍，这是基于这样的假设，即即使新冠肺炎的疫苗出现后，其很大一部分员工仍将继续在家工作。床上用品公司睡眠数字公司计划减缓其总面积的增长，因为越来越多的消费者在网上购物。
道富银行(State Street Corp)首席执行官罗纳德·菲利普·奥汉利(Ronald Philip O‘Hanley)在该公司7月17日的财报电话会议上表示：“你应该预计并将我们的足迹控制在更低的水平，很快就会开始。”
与此同时，地区金融公司(Regions Financial Corp)对分析师表示：“无论是通过增加使用酒店、在家办公还是修改日程安排，我们都有信心总体办公面积将继续下降，”因为一些员工共用办公桌或不再进入大楼。
分析师表示，削减房地产的计划可能是打击上班族的第一波成本削减措施，因为企业试图在可能是一场长期衰退的情况下保持利润率。到目前为止，在大流行期间失去的1470万个美国工作岗位中，大部分都在餐馆、旅游和零售商等受灾严重的地区。
嘉信理财(Charles Schwab)积极股票策略首席投资官比尔·麦克马洪(Bill McMahon)表示，办公支出减少之后，可能会出现裁员和技术投资，这应该有助于在减少劳动力的情况下提高生产率。
他说：“显然，我们看到企业开始对他们的实体工厂进行合理化改造，在某个时候，他们也会转向劳动力。”
摩根士丹利(Morgan Stanley)在6月份预测，在家工作的政策将增加写字楼的空置率。该公司预测，未来两到五年，纽约的空置率将从现在的8.7%升至10%-12%，而旧金山将从5.8%升至7%-9%。
到目前为止，对办公空间使用量下降的担忧并未损害商业抵押贷款支持证券，iShares CMBS ETF今年迄今上涨了4.4%。
独立研究公司Green Street Advisors的分析师丹尼·伊斯梅尔(Danny Ismail)表示，虽然企业往往会在典型的衰退期间削减房地产需求，但过去四个月的经济封锁表明，许多工人可以在家里保持生产力。因此，公司正在进行的削减更有可能是永久性的，他说。
他说：“每家公司都刚刚经历了大规模在家工作的经历，对大多数公司来说，这大多是积极的。”
Green Street Advisors预计，一旦冠状病毒大流行得到控制，由于在家工作的政策，办公室需求将减少高达15%。
伊斯梅尔表示，必要空间的减少很可能会损害在旧金山和纽约等城市有较大敞口的房地产投资公司，因为预计雇主将给予工人更多自由，让他们可以住在远离海岸的低成本地区。
“甚至在新冠肺炎之前，你就已经看到人们从东北和西海岸的大多数沿海城市迁移到太阳带市场，”他说，他指的是从佛罗里达州延伸到南加州的地区，统称为太阳带。“我们认为，随着后新冠肺炎时代的生活成本、生活质量和保住工作的能力比以前好得多，未来这一趋势将会加速。”
(更正为办公室需求将减少高达15%，而不是高达15%的美国上班族可能会保持偏远，第13段。)
note_en:
NEW YORK (Reuters) - Corporate America is downsizing its real estate footprint as companies allow more employees to work from home, a growing threat to the bottom line of owners of traditional office buildings and a sign that companies are looking for ways to cut costs as a result of the coronavirus pandemic.
  A Reuters analysis of quarterly earnings calls over the past week revealed more than 25 large companies plan to reduce their office space in the year ahead, a move designed to reduce the second-largest expense after payrolls at corporations.
 Energy company Halliburton Co said it intends to close more than 100 facilities. Financial services company State Street Corp said it is going to nearly double the workers assigned to one office before adding additional space, based on the assumption that a significant portion of its workforce will continue to work from home even after a vaccine for COVID-19 emerges. Bedding company Sleep Number Corp plans to slow the growth of its total square footage as more consumers shop online.
 “You should expect and hold us to a much lower footprint really starting quite soon,” State Street Corp’s CEO, Ronald Philip O’Hanley, said on the company’s July 17 earnings call.
 Regions Financial Corp, meanwhile, told analysts: “Whether through increased use of hoteling, work from home or modified scheduling, we are confident overall office square footage will continue to decline,” as some workers share desks or stop coming into the building.
 Analysts say the plans to cut back on real estate are likely the first wave of cost-cutting measures to hit office workers as companies try to maintain margins going into what may be a long recession. So far, the majority of the 14.7 million U.S. jobs lost during the pandemic have been in hard-hit areas such as restaurants, travel and retailers.
 Reductions in office spending could likely be followed by layoffs and investments in technology that should help improve productivity with a reduced workforce, said Bill McMahon, chief investment officer of active equity strategies at Charles Schwab.
 “Clearly, we’re seeing businesses starting to rationalize their physical plants and at some point they will turn to labor too,” he said.
 Morgan Stanley in June forecast that work-from-home policies will increase vacancy rates in office buildings. Vacancy rates in New York will reach 10%-12% in the next two to five years from 8.7% now, while San Francisco will reach 7-9% from 5.8%, it predicted.
 So far, concerns about declining office space use have not hurt commercial mortgaged-backed securities, with the iShares CMBS ETF up 4.4% for the year to date.
 While companies tend to cut back on their real estate needs during typical recessions, the last four months of economic lockdown have shown that many workers can remain productive at home, said Danny Ismail, an analyst at independent research firm Green Street Advisors. As a result, the cutbacks that companies are making are more likely to be permanent, he said.
 “Every company just went through a mass work-from-home experience and for the majority it was mostly positive,” he said.
 Green Street Advisors expects that office demand will be reduced by up to 15% as a result of work from home policies once the coronavirus pandemic is contained.
 That reduction in necessary space will most likely hurt real estate investment firms with large exposures in cities such as San Francisco and New York as workers are expected to be given more freedom by employers to live in lower-cost areas away from the coasts, Ismail said.
 “Even before COVID-19, you’d seen a migration from majority coastal cities along the Northeast and West Coast to Sun Belt markets,” he said, referring to the region stretching from Florida to Southern California, collectively known as the Sun Belt. “We think that will accelerate in the future as the cost of living, quality of life and ability to keep your job is much better in a post-COVID-19 world than before.”
 (Corrects to say that office demand will be reduced by up to 15%, instead of that up to 15% of U.S. office workers will likely remain remote, paragraph 13.)
title: 谁还需要这间办公室？美国公司开始削减空间
title_en: Who still needs the office? U.S. companies start cutting space

note_en:
T-Mobile is preparing to make support for   Voice over LTE a requirement for all new devices, according to  a report Thursday. The move was reportedly detailed in internal T-Mobile documents  obtained by Android Police.
 The requirement won't mean much for users at first, but as of January 2021, "T-Mobile will require all devices connecting to our nationwide  4G LTE and  5G networks to be  VoLTE compatible." That means that older-gen devices that don't include support for the IP-based voice network won't be able to use T-Mobile at all.
   VoLTE has been around for several years now, and it enables wireless operators to use the 4G LTE network to transmit voice services in the same way they transmit data. The majority of today's phones  support the feature, including all  iPhones dating back to the  iPhone 6 and all  Samsung Galaxy devices dating back to the  Samsung Galaxy Avant. Those that don't include recent devices from  Xiaomi and the  Huawei P30 Pro.

英特尔今天在其2020年第二季度财报中宣布，与之前计划的发布日期相比，它现在已经将其7纳米CPU的推出推迟了6个月。英特尔的新闻稿还说，其7纳米制程的成品率现在比公司的内部目标晚了12个月，这意味着该公司目前没有能力以经济可行的方式生产7纳米制程。
与之前的预期相比，该公司基于7 nm的CPU产品的推出时间大约有6个月的变动。主要的驱动因素是英特尔7 nm制程的成品率，根据最近的数据，该制程的成品率现在比公司的内部目标低了大约12个月。
目前还不清楚英特尔将如何协调其7 nm处理器延迟6个月与内部7 nm产量预测推迟一年的关系，但该公司的路线图中可能有内置的缓冲。这一延迟反映了又一次挫折，因为英特尔仍在努力克服其10 nm制程遇到的多年成品率问题。这些延迟让AMD等竞争对手在公司历史上第一次抓住机会从英特尔手中夺取了流程节点领导地位。但这在市场上引发了一场价格战，因为英特尔正在用更好的节点与真正的x86竞争对手竞争，更不用说亚马逊基于台积电7 nm节点的新Graviton 2 ARM芯片了。这一声明还加剧了最近有关摇滚明星芯片架构师吉姆·凯勒(Jim Keller)离开公司的消息。
英特尔首席财务官乔治·戴维斯此前曾表示，该公司的制程技术将在2021年之前落后于竞争对手，直到2021年7纳米制程到来，该公司将在未确定的时间重新凭借5纳米制程重新领先：
正如我们在19年5月的分析师日上所说的那样：看，这不仅仅是英特尔有史以来最好的节点。它的生产效率将低于14 nm，低于22 nm，但我们对我们正在看到的改进感到兴奋，我们预计从2021年底开始，7 nm的表现将比7 nm好得多。
由于7纳米的延迟，重新获得竞争地位的计划现在显然已经改变。英特尔曾计划加快其7 nm节点的交付速度，以抵消表现不佳的10 nm节点，英特尔表示，10 nm节点的表现不会像其他节点那样好。当时，戴维斯指出，该公司试图向投资者澄清10纳米对公司毛利率的影响……但事实是，我想弄清楚10纳米时代发生了什么。事实是，它不会像人们从14纳米看到的那样结实，也不会像人们在7 nm看到的那样结实。
此外，为了重新获得工艺领先地位，我们必须加快10 nm、7 nm，然后7 nm和5 nm之间的重叠，因此，特别是从2021年开始，你们重新吸收的成本，10 nm的表现、7 nm的投资，以及5 nm的投资都很好地开始了：所有这些因素加在一起，就会影响毛利率。
这一消息刚刚发布在英特尔的财报中，没有任何预警，该公司表示将在财报电话会议上宣布更多细节，所以我们正在进行更深入的挖掘。该公司不久将举行财报简报会，我们已经联系了进一步的情况。我们将在了解更多信息时更新此帖子。
呀！尽管英特尔可能拥有最好的14 nm制程，但他们的10 nm制程被窃听的时间已经足够长了，以至于落后了。完全控制流程是英特尔的超能力，可以追溯到20世纪80-90年代CISC对RISC的战争。有点可惜...。虽然我在这场赛马中患上AMD已经有很长一段时间了。但我几乎总是喜欢失败者！
回覆。
几年前，当10 nm正在下滑时，英特尔表示，尽管遭遇挫折，但它将在推进10 nm的过程中吸取的教训，对以后推动7 nm是有用的。然而，英特尔仍然没有完全理清10纳米的界限，我猜一些经验教训并没有像英特尔希望的那样延伸到7纳米。看来在2022年之前，英特尔可能不会有太多让人们感到兴奋的东西。
回覆

title_en: Intel says that it has delayed the rollout of its 7nm CPUs by six months, now 12 months behind the company's internal targets

做出快速决定的糟糕的决策者会给自己带来很多麻烦。他们需要放慢脚步，考虑得更周到。
做出缓慢而深思熟虑的决定的糟糕的决策者仍然是糟糕的决策者。他们需要做出更好的决定。
做出缓慢而深思熟虑的决定的好决策者自己做得很好，但由于优柔寡断，他们浪费时间，承受不必要的压力。他们需要更加果断。
决策迅速的好决策者是伟大的决策者。他们相信自己良好的判断力，会做出最正确的决定。
note_en:
Bad decision-makers who make quick decisions get themselves in a lot of trouble. They need to slow down and be more thoughtful.
 Bad decision-makers who make slow and thoughtful decisions are still bad decision-makers. They need to make better decisions.
 Good decision-makers who makes slow and thoughtful decisions do well for themselves but they waste time and endure unnecessary stress due to indecision. They need to be more decisive.
 Good decision-makers who make quick decisions are great decision-makers. They trust their good judgement and make the most good decisions.
title: 如何做出更好的决定
title_en: How to make more good decisions

note:
尽管五角大楼发表声明称，它解散了一个曾经秘密开展的调查不明飞行物的项目，但这项工作仍在进行中--改名后藏在海军情报办公室(Office Of Naval Intelligence)内，那里的官员继续研究军事飞行员与身份不明的飞行器之间令人费解的遭遇。
五角大楼官员不会讨论该项目，该项目不属于机密，但涉及机密事务。然而，它出现在上个月参议院委员会的一份报告中，该报告概述了来年国家情报机构的支出。报告称，这个名为“不明空中现象特遣部队”的项目是为了“标准化收集和报告”目击不明航行器的行为，并将至少每六个月向公众报告部分调查结果。
虽然参与这一行动的退休官员--包括前参议院多数党领袖哈里·里德(Harry Reid)--希望该项目能寻找来自其他世界的车辆的证据，但它的主要关注点是发现另一个国家，特别是任何潜在的对手，是否正在使用可能威胁美国的突破性航空技术。
佛罗里达州共和党参议员、参议院情报特别委员会(Senate Select Committee On Intelligence)代理主席马尔科·卢比奥(Marco Rubio)本月告诉哥伦比亚广播公司(CBS)在迈阿密的一个分支机构，他主要担心有关不明飞机在美国军事基地上空飞行的报道--找出谁应该对此负责符合政府的利益。
他担心中国、俄罗斯或其他一些对手已经取得了“一些技术飞跃”，“允许他们进行这种活动”。
卢比奥说，美国基地上空的一些身份不明的飞行器可能展示了美国军火库没有的技术。但他也指出：“也许对此有一个完全的、某种意义上的、令人厌烦的解释。但我们需要找出答案。“。
2017年，《纽约时报》披露了一个前身单位的存在，名为高级航空航天威胁识别计划(Advanced AerSpace Threat Identify Program)。国防部官员当时表示，该部队及其2200万美元的资金在2012年后已经失效。
然而，参与该项目的人士表示，该项目在2017年及以后仍在运作，这一声明后来得到了国防部的证实。
该项目始于2007年，隶属于国防情报局(Defense Intelligence Agency)，然后被置于负责情报事务的国防部副部长办公室内，国防部仍负责监督该项目。但它与情报界的协调将由海军情报办公室进行，正如参议院预算法案中所描述的那样。在那些年里，该计划从未失效，但关于2017年后的行动几乎没有披露。
五角大楼项目的前主任路易斯·伊利桑多(Luis Elizabeth Ondo)证实，新的特遣部队是从先进的航空航天项目演变而来的。伊利桑多曾是一名军事情报官员，在该项目工作了10年后于2017年10月辞职。
“它再也不用躲在阴影里了，”伊利桑多说。“它将有一个新的透明度。”
伊利桑多是少数拥有安全许可的前政府官员和科学家中的一员，他们表示，他们确信来源不明的物体已经坠落在地球上，带着被带回进行研究的材料，但他们没有出示实物证据。
根据对项目参与者的采访和非机密简报文件，十多年来，五角大楼的项目一直在为国会委员会、航空航天公司高管和其他政府官员进行机密简报。
在某些情况下，已经找到了对以前无法解释的事件的世俗解释。天体物理学家说，即使缺乏可信的地球解释，也不会使外星解释成为最有可能的解释。
里德是来自内华达州的前民主党参议员，他在担任多数党领袖时曾推动资助UFO早先的项目。他说，他认为来自其他世界的车辆发生了撞车事故，几十年来，人们一直在秘密研究被找回的材料，通常是由政府合同下的航空航天公司进行研究。
里德在接受采访时说，“在调查了这件事之后，我得出的结论是，有报道--有些是实质性的，有些不是很实质性的--表明政府和私营部门掌握着实际的材料。”
目前还没有公开生产坠机文物供独立核实。一些被找回的物体，如不寻常的金属碎片，后来从实验室研究中被确认为人造的。
天体物理学家埃里克·W·戴维斯(Eric W.Davis)自2007年以来一直担任五角大楼UFO项目的分包商和顾问，他说，在某些情况下，对这些材料的检查到目前为止未能确定它们的来源，并导致他得出结论，“我们自己做不到。”
对讨论机密项目的限制--以及简报的非机密幻灯片中引用的信息含糊不清--让研究过UFO的官员不得不在没有提出任何确凿证据的情况下发表自己的观点。
戴维斯现在为国防承包商航空航天公司(AerSpace Corporation)工作，他说，最近在3月份，他向国防部的一个机构做了一次机密简报，内容是从“非地球制造的外星飞行器”中回收。
戴维斯说，他还在2019年10月21日向参议院军事委员会的工作人员和两天后的参议院情报委员会成员进行了有关找回不明物体的机密简报。
公众对UFO话题的迷恋吸引了特朗普总统，他告诉儿子小唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump Jr.)。在6月份的一次采访中，他知道罗斯韦尔“非常有趣”的事情-新墨西哥州的一个城市，是猜测UFO存在的中心。当被问及是否会解密有关罗斯威尔的任何信息时，总统表示异议。“我得考虑一下这个问题，”他说。
里德说，无论哪种方式，都应该更多地公开，以澄清哪些是已知的，哪些是未知的。他说：“关于发现物理材料或找回的飞行器的信息公布是极其重要的。”
note_en:
Despite Pentagon statements that it disbanded a once-covert program to investigate unidentified flying objects, the effort remains underway — renamed and tucked inside the Office of Naval Intelligence, where officials continue to study  mystifying encounters between military pilots and unidentified aerial vehicles.
 Pentagon officials will not discuss the program, which is not classified but deals with classified matters. Yet it appeared last month in a  Senate committee report outlining spending on the nation’s intelligence agencies for the coming year. The report said the program, the Unidentified Aerial Phenomenon Task Force, was “to standardize collection and reporting” on sightings of unexplained aerial vehicles, and was to report at least some of its findings to the public every six months.
 While retired officials involved with the effort — including Harry Reid, the former Senate majority leader — hope the program will seek evidence of vehicles from other worlds, its main focus is on discovering whether another nation, especially any potential adversary, is using breakout aviation technology that could threaten the United States.
 Senator Marco Rubio, the Florida Republican who is the acting chairman of the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence,  told a CBS affiliate in Miami this month that he was primarily concerned about reports of unidentified aircraft over American military bases — and that it was in the government’s interest to find out who was responsible.
 He expressed concerns that China or Russia or some other adversary had made “some technological leap” that “allows them to conduct this sort of activity.”
 Mr. Rubio said some of the unidentified aerial vehicles over U.S. bases possibly exhibited technologies not in the American arsenal. But he also noted: “Maybe there is a completely, sort of, boring explanation for it. But we need to find out.”
 In 2017, The New York Times  disclosed the existence of a predecessor unit, called the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program. Defense Department officials said at the time that the unit and its $22 million in funding had lapsed after 2012.
 People working with the program, however, said it was still in operation in 2017 and beyond, statements later confirmed by the Defense Department.
 The program was begun in 2007 under the Defense Intelligence Agency and was then placed within the office of the undersecretary of defense for intelligence, which remains responsible for its oversight. But its coordination with the intelligence community will be carried out by the Office of Naval Intelligence, as described in the Senate budget bill. The program never lapsed in those years, but little was disclosed about the post-2017 operations.
 The Pentagon program’s previous director, Luis Elizondo, a former military intelligence official who resigned in October 2017 after 10 years with the program, confirmed that the new task force evolved from the advanced aerospace program.
  “It no longer has to hide in the shadows,” Mr. Elizondo said. “It will have a new transparency.”
 Mr. Elizondo is among a small group of former government officials and scientists with security clearances who, without presenting physical proof, say they are convinced that objects of undetermined origin have crashed on earth with materials retrieved for study.
 For more than a decade, the Pentagon program has been conducting classified briefings for congressional committees, aerospace company executives and other government officials, according to interviews with program participants and unclassified briefing documents.
 In some cases, earthly explanations have been found for previously unexplained incidents. Even lacking a plausible terrestrial explanation does not make an extraterrestrial one the most likely, astrophysicists say.
 Mr. Reid, the former Democratic senator from Nevada who pushed for funding the earlier U.F.O. program when he was the majority leader, said he believed that crashes of vehicles from other worlds had occurred and that retrieved materials had been studied secretly for decades, often by aerospace companies under government contracts.
 “After looking into this, I came to the conclusion that there were reports — some were substantive, some not so substantive — that there were actual materials that the government and the private sector had in their possession,” Mr. Reid said in an interview.
 No crash artifacts have been publicly produced for independent verification. Some retrieved objects, such as unusual metallic fragments, were later identified from laboratory studies as man-made.
  Eric W. Davis, an astrophysicist who worked as a subcontractor and then a consultant for the Pentagon U.F.O. program since 2007, said that, in some cases, examination of the materials had so far failed to determine their source and led him to conclude, “We couldn’t make it ourselves.”
 The constraints on discussing classified programs — and the ambiguity of information cited in unclassified slides from the briefings — have put officials who have studied U.F.O.s in the position of stating their views without presenting any hard evidence.
 Mr. Davis, who now works for Aerospace Corporation, a defense contractor, said he gave a classified briefing to a Defense Department agency as recently as March about retrievals from “off-world vehicles not made on this earth.”
 Mr. Davis said he also gave classified briefings on retrievals of unexplained objects to staff members of the Senate Armed Services Committee on Oct. 21, 2019, and to members of the Senate Intelligence Committee two days later.
  Public fascination with the topic of U.F.O.s has drawn in President Trump, who told his son Donald Trump Jr. in a June interview that he knew “very interesting” things about Roswell — a city in New Mexico that is central to speculation about the existence of U.F.O.s. The president demurred when asked if he would declassify any information on Roswell. “I’ll have to think about that one,” he said.
 Either way, Mr. Reid said, more should be made public to clarify what is known and what is not. “It is extremely important that information about the discovery of physical materials or retrieved craft come out,” he said.
title: 五角大楼的不明飞行物小组不再处于阴影中，将公布一些发现
title_en: No Longer in Shadows, Pentagon’s UFO Unit Will Make Some Findings Public
